# What Most People Miss About Marketing — Rory Sutherland

*Source: https://youtu.be/YErOtGMgTNg*
*Captions: auto-generated*
*Published: July 21, 2024 — Lenny's Podcast*

---

Rory Sutherland, Vice Chairman of Ogilvy UK and author of *Alchemy*, in conversation with Lenny Rachitsky. Runtime: ~1h 25m.

---

> "All value is perceived value. There is no other kind."

> "Steve Jobs was not a technologist. He was a pitchman, he was a brilliant salesman, he was a fantastic marketer."

> "You can either work out what people want and find a clever way to make it, or you can work out what you can make and find a clever way to make people want it. And actually, the greatest things manage to do both."

> "Having a great brand means you get to play the game of capitalism on easy mode."

> "If you could imagine a stand-up comedian doing a routine about your product, you're onto something. The urge to appear serious is in many ways a disaster in marketing."

> "Don't leave marketing to the last minute — but equally, work in parallel as far as you can."

> "The reason most people adopt new technology is to show off. Rich people provide the early-stage funding for promising ideas before they really reach maturity."

> "We've imbued electric car owners with this holier-than-thou aura, which causes other people to resent them despite the fact that incredibly few of them are really doing this for environmental reasons."

> "Most advanced, yet acceptable. That's the principle. The sweet spot between novelty and familiarity."

> "Try and use those three things — the psychological, the technological, and the economic — in parallel. Because what most businesses do is they try and do things in series."

> "The opposite of a good idea can also be a good idea."

> "Because of the quirks of non-linear, non-proportionate human psychology, it's sometimes possible, with literally no more than a few words, a little nudge, or a little redirection of attention, to turn lead into gold."

> "The means of marketing are abundant. The curiosity to use them is scarce."

> "Metrics-driven workplaces are incredibly demotivating because the measurement strips out all the psychological meaning of the work."
